content.assessment: A compensatory funding and teacher-quality package targets exactly the institutional strain identified in the teacher workforce data: aging demographics and disadvantaged/rural schools bearing 2-3x higher unqualified-teacher proportions. This is one of the few proposals that directly engages a demographic-institutional finding (teacher age structure) rather than only cohort-size or geography. However, because it leaves track structure and selection age unchanged, it does not address the spatial concentration of selective tracks in urban centers nor the mechanical class-size viability problem facing rural schools; disadvantaged rural schools may receive additional funding while still losing viable cohort size for any track type.
opportunities: Teacher incentive and placement funding could be explicitly weighted toward the regions demographically identified as facing compounding institutional strain (rural, low-density, aging teacher workforce), increasing targeting precision
strengths: Directly targets the teacher workforce aging and rural/disadvantaged unqualified-teacher gap, a specific demographic-institutional finding not addressed by any other proposal's core mechanism
Funding-based levers are more adaptable to yearly demographic fluctuation than structural capacity or track-boundary changes, offering flexibility as cohort numbers continue to shift
threats: Absent structural change, continued rural depopulation may erode the pupil base faster than compensatory funding can offset teacher-quality or resource gaps, particularly given the multi-decade, structurally embedded nature of the decline
weaknesses: Does not address the mechanical constraint of statutory minimum class sizes in shrinking rural regions; additional funding cannot manufacture the pupils needed to meet 26/28/34 thresholds where cohorts have already contracted 10-30%

Leaves the urban/rural geographic concentration of selective tracks untouched, so compensatory funding for disadvantaged feeder schools may not reach the specific rural viability crisis described in settlements with only 50-70 school-age children
